intro_band.agent_remit_summary: A single-tenant personal AI agent that assists one operator with open-ended work — conversation, code, a real terminal and browser, scheduled cron jobs, subagent delegation, and cross-session memory — reachable identically across CLI/TUI, a desktop app, an editor adapter, a ~20-platform messaging gateway, and an optional HTTP/API surface. The remit authorizes a broad, operator-gated tool surface (`terminal`, `execute_code`, file tools, `web_search`/`web_extract`, browser, `delegate_task`, `cronjob`, `send_message`, MCP tools) but requires human approval before dangerous shell commands, a caller allowlist (fail-closed) on every enabled messaging/HTTP surface, and an un-overridable refusal of catastrophic commands. It forbids serving unauthenticated callers, emitting telemetry without explicit opt-in, and moving credentials or session-auth material outside the operator's trust envelope. The paired Hermes Desktop is a control surface that must not weaken the Agent's trust rules.
intro_band.agent_structure_summary: A Python LLM-driven agent core (`run_agent.py` / `AIAgent`) with a large `tools/` inventory, a messaging `gateway/` (per-platform adapters), a cron scheduler, and a self-curating memory/skills loop; the paired app is an Electron + React desktop that spawns and drives a headless `hermes serve` backend over loopback JSON-RPC/WS (`hermes-desktop/src/main`). Authorization is enforced at every external surface: `gateway/run.py` default-denies unknown callers, `gateway/platforms/api_server.py` refuses to start without an API key, `hermes_cli/web_server.py` refuses a non-loopback bind without an auth provider. The exec path is guarded by an in-process approval layer (`tools/approval.py`) with an un-overridable hardline blocklist checked before any `--yolo`/`approvals.mode: off` bypass, credential scrubbing into child processes (`tools/env_passthrough.py`), redacting rotating logs (`hermes_logging.py` + `agent/redact.py`), and exact-pinned dependencies with supply-chain CI. `SECURITY.md` is explicit that these in-process controls are heuristics, not containment — OS-level isolation (a non-default terminal backend or whole-process wrapper) is the only boundary against an adversarial LLM, and the operator must choose it. The desktop's own telemetry (`renderer/src/utils/analytics.ts`) is on-by-default for official builds, diverging from the remit's opt-in requirement.

behavior_summary:
This is a mature, actively-hardened agent whose divergences are documented gaps at the edges of an otherwise coherent safety model, not missing foundations. The dominant pattern is the one the remit itself flags as highest-value: the agent ingests attacker-influenceable content (open web, inbound messages, file and MCP responses) into an LLM context that can then drive terminal and execute_code, and on the default local backend the only thing standing between injected model output and host execution is an in-process heuristic approval denylist — real and operative, but by the project's own SECURITY.md not a containment boundary. The genuine boundary (a sandboxed terminal backend or whole-process wrapper) is an operator choice, not a default.

Set against that, the operative controls are strong and consistently applied: fail-closed caller authorization on every messaging and HTTP surface, an un-overridable hardline command floor that survives every bypass mode, a credential blocklist that skills and config cannot override, exact-pinned dependencies with supply-chain CI, and redacting rotating logs. The remaining findings — telemetry defaulting to opt-out, approval-bypass modes, best-effort MCP launch guards, a plaintext desktop key, unstructured action logs — are refinements to a system that has the shape right.

rules.rule_text: No surface should dispatch agent work, resolve approvals, or return output to a caller outside the configured authorization set.
Emit outbound telemetry, usage attribution, analytics, or third-party identifier tagging without an explicit operator opt-in.
Move operator credentials or session-authorization material to any destination outside the operator's trust envelope.
each enabled adapter requires an operator-configured caller allowlist (or DM-pairing approval) before it may serve any caller
Loopback bind is the default; a non-loopback bind must engage authentication.
Password-only auth must not be used for a publicly-exposed backend.

Credentials and gateway tokens MUST NOT be passed into lower-trust child processes (shell subprocesses, execute_code, MCP subprocesses, cron scripts) except variables the operator or a loaded skill has explicitly declared as passthrough.
Secrets MUST NOT be written to logs, transcripts, error messages returned to the model, or any outbound adapter payload.
Credentials MUST NOT be committed to version control or stored in the main (shareable) config file.

rules.section: Action Boundaries — Requires Human Approval Before Execution
rules.rule_text: Any terminal command matching a dangerous/destructive pattern on a host-reaching backend MUST prompt the operator and MUST NOT execute until approved.

rules.section: Action Boundaries — Requires Human Approval Before Execution
rules.rule_text: When an approval prompt is not answered within the configured window, the action MUST be denied (fail closed).

rules.section: Action Boundaries — Requires Human Approval Before Execution
rules.rule_text: Installing or loading a third-party skill or plugin MUST give the operator a chance to review its actual code before it executes.

rules.section: Action Boundaries — Requires Human Approval Before Execution
rules.rule_text: Cron jobs that hit a dangerous-command prompt MUST default to denying it rather than silently auto-approving.

rules.section: Action Boundaries — Requires Human Approval Before Execution
rules.rule_text: Binding any local-only surface to a non-loopback interface MUST be a deliberate operator action that engages an authentication layer.

Executing a catastrophic, irreversible command (filesystem-root wipe, fork bomb, formatting a mounted device, zeroing a physical disk, piping an untrusted remote script straight to a shell) MUST be refused unconditionally — even under --yolo, approvals.mode: off, a permanent allowlist entry, or headless cron approve-mode.

Dispatching agent work, resolving an approval, or returning agent output to a caller who is outside the configured authorization set (allowlist, pairing approval, or OS-level equivalent) MUST NOT be possible; treating a session identifier as proof of authorization is forbidden.
A network-exposed adapter MUST NOT fail open (serve callers) when no allowlist / auth provider is configured.
Credentials or session-authorization material MUST NOT leak to any destination outside the operator's trust envelope through environment leakage, adapter logging, or transport error paths.

The desktop app MUST NOT weaken the Agent's trust rules: it MUST rely on the same backend authorization (auth gate on non-loopback binds, allowlists) and MUST NOT expose a password-only-protected remote backend to the public internet.

The desktop-launched backend MUST remain a JSON-RPC/API surface only (no unintended browser-reachable UI) and MUST stay bound to loopback unless the operator deliberately configures a remote/authenticated setup.
Agent actions and decisions are recorded to durable, operator-inspectable logs.
Sessions are isolated from one another; one session MUST NOT read another session's data or state.
a command that was denied or blocked MUST NOT be silently retried or rephrased to evade the guard.

findings.summary: On the default local backend, attacker-influenceable input reaching the LLM can drive host shell/code execution gated only by an in-process heuristic approval denylist.
findings.description: The remit's own top risk sensitivity — untrusted content (open web, inbound messages, file and MCP responses) entering an LLM context that can then call `terminal` / `execute_code` — is realized on the default host-reaching backend, where the only interposition is the approval layer's regex denylist over a Turing-complete shell, which `SECURITY.md` explicitly declines to treat as a boundary. The genuine boundary (a sandboxed terminal backend or whole-process wrapper) exists but is an operator choice, not the default. Real mitigations blunt the chain — the un-overridable hardline floor, credential scrubbing into child processes, and a default-on approval prompt — so this is a serious gap rather than an open door.
policy_rule_text: Any terminal command matching a dangerous/destructive pattern on a host-reaching backend MUST prompt the operator and MUST NOT execute until approved.

evidence.snippet: "The only security boundary against an adversarial LLM is the operating system... not the approval gate, not any pattern scanner" — in-process controls are heuristics; OS isolation is the operator's choice, default local backend is host-reaching.

dangerous-command approval is a regex denylist (DANGEROUS_PATTERNS) over shell strings; below the hardline floor it is bypassable and, per SECURITY.md §2.4, structurally incomplete against adversarial output.

recommended_actions: Document that operators ingesting untrusted input MUST select an OS-level isolation posture (non-default terminal backend or whole-process wrapper), and consider defaulting untrusted-input deployments (gateway/email enabled) to a sandboxed backend rather than the local host backend.
Resolve Open Question 2 in the remit for this deployment (which terminal backend is sanctioned while ingesting untrusted input) and encode it as an enforced configuration check at startup.

findings.summary: Hermes Desktop telemetry is on-by-default (opt-out) for official builds and tags a persistent anonymous identifier, contrary to the remit's explicit opt-in requirement.
findings.description: The remit forbids emitting telemetry, analytics, or third-party identifier tagging without an explicit operator opt-in, but `analytics.ts` enables PostHog on first run whenever a build-time key is present (`stored === null` returns `hasKey`), which is an opt-out model for official builds. It mints a persistent anonymous UUID and calls `posthog.identify(...)` with app version, platform, and node version to PostHog EU. Privacy hardening (DNT-respect, no session recording, PII masking) and a likely-restrictive renderer CSP reduce the impact, but the default posture diverges from the stated intent.
policy_rule_text: Emit outbound telemetry, usage attribution, analytics, or third-party identifier tagging without an explicit operator opt-in.

evidence.snippet: isAnalyticsEnabled() — "const hasKey = POSTHOG_KEY.length > 0; ... if (stored === null) return hasKey" — first run enabled when a key is baked in (opt-out), not opt-in.
posthog.identify(getOrCreateAnonymousId(), {app_version, platform, node_version}) — persistent localStorage UUID + environment fingerprint sent to eu.i.posthog.com.

recommended_actions: Change the default in `isAnalyticsEnabled()` so telemetry is disabled until the operator explicitly consents (`stored === null` should return false), making the consent model opt-in as the remit requires.
Surface a first-run consent prompt before any `posthog.capture`/`identify` call fires.

findings.summary: Approval-bypass modes (--yolo, approvals.mode=off, permanent allowlist, cron approve-mode) collapse the per-command approval to the hardline floor only, leaving recoverable-but-destructive commands ungated.
findings.description: When any bypass mode is active, `approve_command` short-circuits before the dangerous-command prompt, so every non-hardline destructive command (e.g. `rm -rf /tmp/x`, `git reset --hard`, `curl | sh`) runs without confirmation, and a cron session in approve-mode can auto-approve them unattended. The hardline floor still holds, so catastrophic commands remain blocked, but this is standing autonomy without oversight for the large middle tier of dangerous operations. Whether these modes are permitted at all is the remit's Open Question 3 for this deployment.
policy_rule_text: Cron jobs that hit a dangerous-command prompt MUST default to denying it rather than silently auto-approving.

evidence.snippet: "if _YOLO_MODE_FROZEN or is_current_session_yolo_enabled():" — bypass short-circuits after the hardline check but before the dangerous-command approval prompt; repeated at :1197 and :1487 with `approval_mode == "off"`.

recommended_actions: For cron/headless sessions, default dangerous (non-hardline) commands to deny even under approve-mode, matching remit rule R-13, and require an explicit per-job opt-in to auto-approve.
Emit an operator alert (per the remit's escalation rules) whenever a session activates YOLO/approvals-off so the bypass is visible.

findings.summary: MCP launch guards are best-effort — the OSV malware pre-check fails open on any network error and the tool-description injection scan only warns rather than blocking.
findings.description: Before launching an npx/uvx MCP server, `osv_check` queries OSV for malware advisories but returns None (allow) on any timeout, network error, or parse failure, so an unreachable OSV endpoint lets an unvetted package launch. Separately, `_scan_mcp_description` detects instruction-like (tool-poisoning) content in MCP tool descriptions but only logs a warning — a poisoned description still reaches the model. Both are reasonable defaults for an operator-installed, PR-reviewed MCP catalog, but they are advisory, not enforcing.

tags.label: Tool descriptions contain no hidden model instructions

evidence.snippet: "except Exception ... # Fail-open: network errors, timeouts, parse failures → allow" — malware pre-check does not block when OSV is unreachable.
_scan_mcp_description scans tool descriptions for injection patterns but logs a warning only (no block), so a tool-poisoning description still enters model context.

recommended_actions: Make the OSV malware check fail closed (or prompt the operator) for network-installed MCP packages instead of allowing on error, or cache last-known-good results.
Escalate a positive `_scan_mcp_description` hit to an operator approval gate before the poisoned tool is exposed to the model.

findings.summary: The Hermes Desktop remote-backend API key is written in plaintext to ~/.hermes/desktop.json with no OS-keychain / safeStorage encryption at rest.
findings.description: `setConnectionConfig` serializes the remote API key straight into `desktop.json` as JSON, relying only on filesystem permissions rather than Electron `safeStorage` or the OS keychain. This is session-authorization material the remit treats as sensitive; it is not committed to version control and never crosses to the renderer (both good), but at-rest encryption is the missing hardening. Handling of sudo/askpass passwords is stronger — those stay in memory over a 0o600 socket and are never persisted.

evidence.snippet: "data.remoteApiKey = config.apiKey;" then writeFileSync(desktopConfigFile(), JSON.stringify(data, null, 2)) at :63 — remote key persisted as plaintext JSON.

recommended_actions: Encrypt the remote API key at rest using Electron `safeStorage` (OS keychain-backed) before writing `desktop.json`, decrypting only in the main process when establishing the connection.

findings.summary: Agent action/decision logs are durable and redacted but written as free-form formatted text, not a structured event stream, limiting automated detection.
findings.description: `hermes_logging` records activity to rotating, redacted, session-tagged files, which satisfies the remit's durable operator-inspectable-logs requirement, but the format is a human-readable log line (`%(asctime)s %(levelname)s ... %(message)s`) rather than schema'd JSON events with per-action/decision event types. That is inspectable by a human but not readily consumable by automated detection rules or anomaly detection, which is the gap that holds Monitor Continuously at Partial.

evidence.snippet: "_LOG_FORMAT = \"%(asctime)s %(levelname)s%(session_tag)s %(name)s: %(message)s\"" — free-form text log lines; no structured/JSON event schema or per-action event types.

recommended_actions: Add a structured (JSON-lines) action/decision audit log alongside the human-readable logs, emitting typed events (tool_call, approval_decision, auth_deny, cron_run) so automated detection rules can be written against it.

positives.title: Un-overridable hardline command floor
positives.description: A tiny blocklist of catastrophic, irreversible commands (root/system-dir/home recursive delete, mkfs, dd to raw block device, fork bomb, kill -1, shutdown/reboot) is checked before any bypass mode, so it cannot be defeated by `--yolo`, `approvals.mode: off`, a permanent allowlist, or cron approve-mode.

positives.title: Fail-closed authorization on every external surface
positives.description: Messaging adapters default-deny unknown callers, the API server refuses to start without a key (even on loopback), and the dashboard refuses a non-loopback bind without an auth provider; a session id is never accepted as proof of authorization and is re-checked against the allowlist.

positives.title: Un-overridable credential-scrubbing blocklist
positives.description: Provider API keys and gateway tokens are stripped from shell/execute_code/MCP child environments, and skills or config.yaml cannot register a Hermes-managed provider credential as passthrough — the blocklist that closed GHSA-rhgp-j443-p4rf cannot itself be overridden.

positives.title: Exact-pinned dependencies with supply-chain CI
positives.description: Every direct dependency is pinned to `==X.Y.Z` with CVE annotations, backed by a lockfile, OSV-scanner and supply-chain-audit workflows, a uv-lockfile-check, and SHA-pinned GitHub Actions.

positives.title: Redacting, rotating, session-tagged logs
positives.description: All log files use a RotatingFileHandler with a RedactingFormatter that masks secrets; redaction is on by default and snapshotted at import so LLM-emitted env mutation cannot disable it mid-session, and every record carries a session tag for correlation.

positives.title: Approval prompts fail closed on timeout
positives.description: When a dangerous-command approval prompt is not answered within the configured window, the request is denied rather than left pending or auto-approved.

positives.title: Memory-write injection scanning and drift detection
positives.description: Writes to persistent memory are scanned under the strict threat-pattern scope and refused on match, snapshots are sanitized before entering context, and on-disk drift is detected and refused — reducing the memory-poisoning persistence surface.

positives.title: Desktop Electron hardening and credential renderer-isolation
positives.description: The desktop app runs with contextIsolation and sandbox on and nodeIntegration off, constrains navigation and webviews to a loopback allowlist through a single external-URL choke point, and never exposes the remote API key to the renderer.

rows.purpose: main INFO+ activity log for all agent, tool, and session events
warnings and errors only, for quick triage
gateway-only events — platform adapters, session mgmt, delivery

raise_posture.weighted_rationale: Partial, at the boundary with Established. Hermes enforces genuine, consistently-applied controls where it matters most — fail-closed authorization at every external surface, an un-overridable catastrophic-command floor, a credential-scrubbing blocklist that cannot be overridden by skills or config, and a well-managed supply chain (exact pins, lockfiles, OSV and supply-chain CI, SHA-pinned Actions). What holds the overall at Partial rather than higher is the category the framework weights double: the external-input-to-host-execution path is gated, by default, only by an in-process heuristic the project itself declines to call a boundary, with real OS isolation left to operator choice. Observability is durable and redacted but unstructured, and adversarial testing is present as regression tests and advisory-driven fixes rather than an ongoing red-team practice.

categories.name: Limit Your Domain
categories.rationale: Hermes is a general-purpose personal agent (terminal, code, browser, ~20 messaging platforms) — broad by design — but the breadth is genuinely scoped: single-tenant with no in-instance privilege brokering, explicit enumerated Non-Goals, per-platform operator-configured toolsets via `hermes tools`, and a hardline floor that bounds what even a jailbroken session can do to the host. The domain is wide and enforced structurally rather than narrowed, so it earns Partial, not more.

categories.name: Balance Your Knowledge Base
categories.rationale: External content (web, inbound messages, file reads, MCP responses) necessarily enters the LLM context, and there are real controls — `tools/threat_patterns.py` scanning of context/memory/tool-results, invisible-unicode detection, SSRF/URL safety, and strict-scope scanning plus drift detection on memory writes — but tool-result scanning is warn-only by design and untrusted content still reaches an exec-capable context, so coverage is partial. No vector/embedding store exists, so LLM08 does not apply.

categories.name: Implement Zero Trust
categories.rationale: The trust boundary is enforced with real code, consistently: every messaging and HTTP surface default-denies unknown callers and re-checks authorization rather than trusting a session id (`gateway/run.py`, `gateway/platforms/api_server.py`), the hardline command floor is checked before any bypass mode (`tools/approval.py`), unanswered approvals fail closed, and provider credentials cannot be registered as sandbox passthrough (`tools/env_passthrough.py`). The known, accepted gap is that the input-to-exec path on the default local backend rests on a heuristic approval denylist, with OS isolation left to operator choice.

categories.name: Manage Your Supply Chain
categories.rationale: Every direct dependency is exact-pinned to `==X.Y.Z` with CVE annotations in `pyproject.toml`, backed by `uv.lock`, an OSV scanner workflow, a supply-chain-audit workflow, a uv-lockfile-check, and SHA-pinned GitHub Actions; MCP package launches get an OSV malware pre-check and the MCP catalog requires pinned refs. This is comprehensive, actively-managed supply-chain hygiene — Strong, held below Exemplary only by the fail-open OSV check and warn-only tool-description scan.

categories.name: Build an AI Red Team
categories.rationale: There is genuine adversarial-testing evidence — a large security-focused regression suite (auth-bypass, fail-closed, injection-pattern tests) and architectural fixes driven by disclosed advisories (the `_HERMES_PROVIDER_ENV_BLOCKLIST` exists because of GHSA-rhgp-j443-p4rf; the skills-guard multi-word-bypass fix) — but this reads as security regression testing and coordinated disclosure rather than an ongoing proactive red-team practice, so Partial.

categories.name: Monitor Continuously
categories.rationale: Actions and decisions are recorded to durable, redacted, rotating, session-tagged logs with component routing (`hermes_logging.py` + `agent/redact.py`), which is real and operator-inspectable — but the format is free-form formatted text rather than a structured/schema'd event stream, so it does not readily support automated detection rules, holding the category at Partial.
